        The sun hung like a white-hot coin above the endless sea of sand. Kael adjusted the strap of his pack, his fingers numb from the cold sweat that had long since dried on his skin. Every step was a negotiation with gravity, a battle against the shifting dunes that seemed to swallow the horizon whole. He had been walking for three days, the water in his canteen reduced to a sludge of dust and despair.

        The heat waves danced before his eyes, promising oases that dissolved the moment he reached them. He remembered the radio transmission from the base camp, the voice crackling with static before cutting out. They had said they would send a team. They had said they would come. But the silence of the desert was a heavy blanket, and it pressed down on his chest with the weight of a thousand unspoken truths.

        He stopped at the edge of a rocky outcrop, shielding his eyes from the glare. The wind howled through the crevices, sounding like a warning. He knew that rescue was a myth in this place. The only thing that mattered was the next step, the next breath, the next drop of water. He tightened his grip on the walking stick and began to move again, toward the faint line of green that might have been a mirage, or might have been the only hope left.
